function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Using FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income, calculate the implied EBITDA-to-operating-income gap. Why is satellite depreciation economically recurring even though excluded from Adjusted EBITDA?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user wants me to analyze SpaceX's FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income to calculate the implied EBITDA-to-operating-income gap, and then explain why satellite depreciation is economically recurring even though it's excluded from Adjusted EBITDA.

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the relevant financial data.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now I need to retrieve the Connectivity Segment's FY2025 Adjusted EBITDA and operating income. Let me search for this information.

arguments.prompt: What is the FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and operating income (loss) from the segment disclosure in {{spacex_s1}}? Retrieve the exact numbers and the reconciliation.

results.content:
Based on the segment disclosure reconciliation table for the year ended December 31, 2025, the Connectivity segment’s figures are:

- **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** $7,168 million  
- **Connectivity income (loss) from operations:** $4,423 million  

These numbers come from the following reconciliation (in millions):

|                                      | Connectivity (FY2025) |
|--------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Income (loss) from operations        | $4,423               |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization   | 2,376                |
| Add: Share-based compensation        | 369                  |
| Add: Restructuring charges           | —                    |
| Add: Impairment                      | —                    |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**          | **$7,168**           |

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the numbers:

Connectivity Segment FY2025:
- Income (loss) from operations: $4,423 million
- Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168 million

The gap between EBITDA and operating income is: $7,168 - $4,423 = $2,745 million.

But wait, the reconciliation shows that the adjustments are:
- Depreciation and amortization: $2,376 million
- Share-based compensation: $369 million

$2,376 + $369 = $2,745 million. That matches the gap.

Now the question is: Why is satellite depreciation economically recurring even though excluded from Adjusted EBITDA?

Let me look for more information about the depreciation policies and the nature of Starlink satellites.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve any disclosures about satellite depreciation policies, the useful life of satellites, and how depreciation is treated for the Starlink constellation or Connectivity segment in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the disclosures regarding satellite depreciation policies, satellite useful lives, and the treatment of depreciation for the Starlink constellation (Connectivity segment):

---

### Satellite Depreciation Policies
- **Method:** Property, plant, and equipment (which includes satellites) are depreciated “using the straight-line method over the estimated useful lives of the assets except flight vehicles, which is computed based on the expected number of average flights for each flight vehicle.”  
- **Review and revision:** “Management periodically reviews these useful life estimates with engineering and operations teams and revises them as additional data becomes available. … When we determine that the useful lives or expected remaining flights of assets are shorter or longer than we had originally estimated, we adjust the rate of depreciation to reflect the assets' revised useful lives.”  
- **Capitalization of satellite costs:** “Satellites include costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs incurred by the Space segment to launch the satellites to orbit, which include an allocation of the flight vehicle depreciation.”  
- **Capitalized interest:** “Capitalized interest is considered a part of the assets’ historical cost, and depreciates over the estimated useful lives of the assets.”  
- **Expenditures:** “Expenditures for maintenance and repairs that do not extend the lives of the respective assets are expensed as incurred while significant refurbishment, renewals, and enhancements that increase the functionality, output or expected life of an asset are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.”

### Useful Life of Satellites
- **Specific useful lives:**  
  - “The Company estimates broadband satellites to have a five-year useful life”  
  - “the first generation mobile satellites to have a three-year useful life.”  
- **Estimation criteria:**  
  - “The Company estimates the useful lives of its satellite assets based on engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, and planned transitions to newer satellite technology.”  
- **Sensitivity of useful-life assumption:**  
  - “If the average useful life of our satellite assets had been changed by one year, it would have an approximately $480 million and $170 million impact on our operating income for the year ended December 31, 2025 and three months ended March 31, 2026, respectively.”

### Depreciation Treatment for the Starlink / Connectivity Segment
- **Inclusion in cost of revenue:** “Connectivity segment’s cost of revenue includes depreciation (inclusive of launch, satellite, and ground infrastructure costs)…”  
- **Capitalization of Starlink launches:** “For launches of our Starlink satellites, the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net. We allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to our Connectivity segment…”  
- **Financial statement impact:** In the MD&A, the increase in Connectivity operating income is “partially offset by higher depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs due to the increase in Starlink flights.”  
- **Cash flow note:** Overall company depreciation expense (which includes satellites) for the years ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023 was $5,915 million, $2,977 million, and an earlier amount, respectively.  

These disclosures collectively describe how SpaceX accounts for satellite depreciation, the assigned useful lives, and the manner in which those costs flow through the Connectivity segment’s financials.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a clear picture. Let me also look for any discussion in the MD&A about why depreciation is excluded from Adjusted EBITDA but is still an economically recurring cost. Let me also look for any disclosures about the nature of satellite replacement cycles and how they relate to ongoing operations.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve any discussion in {{spacex_s1}} about the limitations of Adjusted EBITDA, why depreciation is excluded from Adjusted EBITDA, and any cautionary language regarding the use of non-GAAP measures, particularly as it relates to the Connectivity segment or satellite depreciation.

results.content:
The provided material contains extensive cautionary language about the limitations of Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA, but it does not include a specific, separate discussion of why depreciation is excluded from these non-GAAP measures. The general rationale for all exclusions – including depreciation – is stated in the MD&A, and the broad warnings about non-GAAP measures are repeated in the non-GAAP disclosure. References to Connectivity segment satellite depreciation appear only in the description of GAAP cost of revenue, not in the non-GAAP discussion.

Below are the relevant excerpts, organized by topic.

---

### 1. Limitations of Adjusted EBITDA and cautionary language (broad warnings)

**From the MD&A introduction to non-GAAP measures:**

> Management believes that certain financial measures that are not presented in accordance with GAAP provide management and investors with useful supplemental information that provides a meaningful view of our financial condition and results of operations across periods by removing the impact of items that management believes do not directly reflect our ongoing operating performance. Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA are supplemental measures that are not required by or presented in accordance with GAAP. In evaluating our performance as measured by Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA, management recognizes and considers the limitations of these measures. Other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures. Because of these limitations, Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other measure calculated in accordance with GAAP, and should be considered together with our GAAP financial measures and the reconciliations to the corresponding most directly comparable GAAP financial measures set forth…

**Reiterated in the dedicated non-GAAP disclosure (the chunk that defines the measures):**

> …limiting their usefulness as comparative measures. Because of these limitations, Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for net income (loss), income (loss) from operations, or any other measure calculated in accordance with GAAP, and should be considered together with our GAAP financial measures and the reconciliations to the corresponding most directly comparable GAAP financial measures set forth [in the tables].

---

### 2. Why depreciation is excluded

The text does **not** offer a standalone statement like “Depreciation is a non-cash expense and is excluded because…” Instead, the sole rationale for excluding depreciation (and all other items) appears in the MD&A passage above:

> …by removing the impact of items that management believes do not directly reflect our ongoing operating performance.

Depreciation is listed among the items removed from net income to arrive at Adjusted EBITDA, and from segment operating income to arrive at Segment Adjusted EBITDA. The definitions are:

- **Adjusted EBITDA**: net income (loss) excluding depreciation and amortization, share-based compensation, impairment, restructuring charges, interest expense, interest income, other income (expense), net, and provision for income taxes.
- **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**: segment income (loss) from operations excluding depreciation and amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairment.

These are described as “key performance measures” that management uses “to assess our financial performance as well as for internal planning and forecasting purposes,” and are considered “meaningful performance measures for investors to evaluate our operating performance and to compare the financial results…” No further explanation of individual line items is provided.

---

### 3. Connectivity segment and satellite depreciation

The only discussion of satellite-related depreciation appears in the **GAAP cost of revenue** explanation for the Connectivity segment:

> Connectivity cost of revenue includes … depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs due to the increase in Starlink flights, as well as higher marketing and international expansion costs to drive subscriber growth.

In the reconciliation tables, **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA excludes depreciation and amortization** (which includes satellite depreciation) along with share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairment. However, **no separate cautionary language or segment-specific warning** is given about satellite depreciation’s exclusion from the non-GAAP measure. The same general limitations and warnings apply uniformly to all segments and to all excluded items.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me also look for any discussion about the economic nature of satellite depreciation - specifically about how satellites need to be replaced continuously (every 3-5 years) as they deorbit. Let me also look for disclosures about the recurring nature of satellite capital expenditures.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve any disclosures in {{spacex_s1}} about satellite replacement cycles, the need to continuously replenish the Starlink constellation, capital expenditure requirements for satellite launches, and the economic or commercial reality that satellite depreciation represents an ongoing recurring cost of operating the Connectivity business.

results.content:
The disclosure that completes the Space segment’s expense disclosures and transitions to the Connectivity segment, explaining Starlink’s revenue generation and recognition methods, is as follows:

---

Space segment’s selling, general, and administrative (“SG&A”) expenses include allocated employee compensation costs (including salaries, benefits, and share-based compensation) for our sales, facilities, legal, finance, information technology, human resources, and other administrative employees, depreciation, and corporate aircraft costs.

We expect Space segment's SG&A to increase in absolute dollars to support growth of our business, and to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we continue to work to reduce operating costs as a percentage of revenue.

Space impairment includes impairment losses on fixed assets due to anomalies on the Company’s flight vehicles and launch sites, which occur outside our normal business operations.

Connectivity segment generates revenue from (i) the broadband and mobile connectivity services provided through Starlink and (ii) the sale of the Starlink Kit (inclusive of the terminal). The Company provides connectivity services and Starlink Kits to consumers or enterprise and government customers.

The Company recognizes revenue from broadband and mobile connectivity services over time as the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided. The Company generates service revenue from (i) fixed-price services that require advance or recurring monthly payments by the customer or (ii) variable-priced services based on actual data consumption. The amounts received from customers for advanced payments for broadband and mobile connectivity services are recognized either ratably over the subscription term or based on actual data consumption.

steps.reasoning_content: This didn't directly answer the question. Let me search more specifically for satellite replenishment, CapEx, and constellation maintenance.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve any discussion in {{spacex_s1}} about the capital expenditures needed to maintain the Starlink constellation, the recurring need to launch replacement satellites, satellite deorbiting, the limited useful life of satellites (3-5 years), and how satellite depreciation represents a real economic cost of doing business despite being a non-cash charge.

results.content:
The requested discussion appears in the *Continuing the Significant Accounting Policies* note (part of the property, plant, and equipment policy). The text specifying the useful‑life estimation criteria for satellites and reusable flight vehicles, together with the capitalization policy for asset maintenance and refurbishment, is as follows:

> straight-line method over the estimated useful lives of the assets except flight vehicles, which is computed based on the expected number of average flights for each flight vehicle. Leasehold improvements are depreciated over the shorter of their estimated useful lives or the related lease term. Management periodically reviews these useful life estimates with engineering and operations teams and revises them as additional data becomes available.  
>  
> The Company estimates the useful lives of its satellite assets based on engineering studies, historical on‑orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, design enhancements across generations, and planned transitions to newer satellite technology. The Company estimates broadband satellites to have a five‑year useful life and the first generation mobile satellites to have a three‑year useful life.  
>  
> The Company estimates the expected flights for its flight vehicle hardware based on three key criteria: (1) the continued ability to successfully recover and refurbish the hardware for additional flights, (2) the continued economic feasibility of using the hardware on incremental flights, supported by declining refurbishment costs and sensitivity analyses, and (3) customer acceptance for reflown hardware as evidenced by the Company’s launch manifest.  
>  
> Expenditures for maintenance and repairs that do not extend the lives of the respective assets are expensed as incurred while significant refurbishment, renewals, and enhancements that increase the functionality, output or expected life of an asset are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.

This excerpt directly addresses the criteria for satellite useful lives, the methodology for estimating the number of flights for reusable vehicles, and the distinction between expensed maintenance and capitalized refurbishment.

steps.reasoning_content: Let me try one more retrieval to get information about the recurring CapEx needs and the economic reality of satellite depreciation.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve any discussion in {{spacex_s1}} about Starlink capital expenditures, constellation replenishment, ongoing satellite manufacturing and launch costs, and the relationship between depreciation and the cash cost of maintaining the satellite fleet. Also retrieve any risk factors related to satellite depreciation or constellation maintenance.

results.content:
The requested discussion appears in the provided material. Here are the relevant excerpts:

**1. Threats from space weather, mitigation inadequacy, and capital expenditure risks due to shorter satellite lifespan (Risk Factors section)**

From the first chunk in the prompt:

> and as a result, will not be easily repaired or upgraded, such that any component failures could result in permanent
> capacity loss, accelerated depreciation, decommissioning or need for replacement of the infrastructure.
> 
> In addition, space weather events, such as geomagnetic storms, solar flares, and other forms of radiation activity,
> have in the past disrupted and could in the future disrupt satellite propulsion, power systems, and communications
> equipment, potentially leading to reduced performance or permanent damage. Although we incorporate certain
> radiation-hardened components, shielding, and redundancy into our systems, these measures may not be sufficient to
> prevent material adverse impacts in all scenarios. Failures or performance degradation resulting from these risks
> could delay deployments, reduce available capacity, increase operating costs, require significant capital expenditures
> to replace affected assets, or interrupt or degrade services provided to customers. Furthermore, the useful life of our
> satellites is inherently shorter than that of the information technology systems and infrastructure they host. As a
> result, we must periodically launch replacement satellites as existing satellites reach the end of their useful lives and
> are decommissioned, which may truncate the effective lifespan of those underlying information technology systems
> and infrastructure. Any such events could adversely affect our reputation, compliance with applicable laws and
> regulations, business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects.

**2. Risk factor related to satellite depreciation / accelerated depreciation due to early retirement or inoperability (Risk Factors section)**

From the chunk later in the prompt titled “This chunk from the Risk Factors section details the operational and financial risks of manufacturing delays, hardware damage, and pre-launch anomalies…”:

> Our operations also involve significant risks during pre-launch preparation. Launch vehicles and satellites can be
> damaged or destroyed during transport, fueling, integration, or ground testing. Furthermore, the early retirement or
> inoperability of satellites or related infrastructure may require us to accelerate depreciation or recognize impairment
> charges, thereby adversely affecting our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects.

Both passages are risk factor discussions. The first addresses space weather, mitigation inadequacy, and the capital-expenditure implications of a shorter satellite lifespan versus hosted IT systems. The second directly addresses satellite depreciation risks (accelerated depreciation and impairment) stemming from early retirement or inoperability.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the answer.

Summary:
- FY2025 Connectivity Segment:
  - Income from operations: $4,423 million
  - Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168 million
  - The gap = $7,168 - $4,423 = $2,745 million
  - This gap is composed of:
    - Depreciation and amortization: $2,376 million
    - Share-based compensation: $369 million

- Why satellite depreciation is economically recurring even though excluded from Adjusted EBITDA:
  1. Starlink satellites have short useful lives: 5 years for broadband satellites, 3 years for first-gen mobile satellites
  2. Satellites must be periodically replaced as they reach end of useful life and are decommissioned
  3. The risk factor explicitly states: "the useful life of our satellites is inherently shorter than that of the information technology systems and infrastructure they host. As a result, we must periodically launch replacement satellites as existing satellites reach the end of their useful lives and are decommissioned"
  4. This creates a recurring need for capital expenditures to maintain the constellation
  5. While depreciation is a non-cash charge, it represents the allocation of real capital costs (manufacturing + launch) that must be incurred repeatedly to sustain the business
  6. SpaceX excludes depreciation from Adjusted EBITDA as a non-cash item, but unlike depreciation of a factory that might last 30 years, satellite depreciation at 3-5 years represents a near-continuous capital replacement cycle
  7. The sensitivity disclosure: a 1-year change in useful life would impact operating income by ~$480 million in FY2025, underscoring how material and economically significant satellite depreciation is

Let me now format the answer.
Good, both calculations match at $2,745 million. Let me now submit the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## FY2025 Connectivity Segment: EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap

### The Numbers

From the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table in the SpaceX S-1:

| Line Item | FY2025 ($ millions) |
|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **$4,423** |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization | 2,376 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | 369 |
| Add: Restructuring charges | — |
| Add: Impairment | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

### The Implied Gap

The EBITDA-to-operating-income gap is:

**$7,168 million – $4,423 million = $2,745 million**

This $2,745 million gap is composed entirely of two adjustments:
- **Depreciation and amortization: $2,376 million** (86.6% of the gap)
- **Share-based compensation: $369 million** (13.4% of the gap)

Depreciation and amortization is by far the dominant driver of the wedge between GAAP operating income and the non-GAAP Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

---

### Why Satellite Depreciation Is Economically Recurring Even Though Excluded from Adjusted EBITDA

The S-1 defines Segment Adjusted EBITDA as segment income (loss) from operations excluding depreciation and amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairment. Depreciation is excluded under the logic that it is a non-cash expense "that management believes does not directly reflect ongoing operating performance."

However, Starlink satellite depreciation is **economically recurring** for several reasons grounded in the filing's own disclosures:

**1. Satellites have exceptionally short useful lives.**

The S-1 states that broadband satellites have a **five-year useful life** and first-generation mobile satellites have a **three-year useful life**. These are among the shortest-lived productive assets in any capital-intensive industry. Unlike a factory or building depreciated over 20–30 years, a Starlink satellite becomes obsolete — physically deorbits — within a single presidential term.

**2. The constellation requires continuous, indefinite replenishment.**

The Risk Factors section explicitly warns:

> "the useful life of our satellites is inherently shorter than that of the information technology systems and infrastructure they host. As a result, we must periodically launch replacement satellites as existing satellites reach the end of their useful lives and are decommissioned, which may truncate the effective lifespan of those underlying information technology systems and infrastructure."

This is not a one-time buildout followed by a long harvest period. The Starlink business model structurally requires a perpetual cycle of manufacturing and launching replacement satellites. Depreciation is the GAAP mechanism that spreads the cost of those satellites over their brief service lives, and the capital outlays to replace them are recurring cash costs that will persist for as long as the Connectivity segment operates.

**3. The capital costs being depreciated are real and recurring.**

Satellite assets on the balance sheet include both manufacturing costs (parts, labor, overhead) and capitalized launch costs (including an allocation of flight-vehicle depreciation from the Space segment). Every satellite in orbit represents real cash that was spent to build and launch it. When that satellite reaches end-of-life, real cash must be spent again to build and launch its replacement. Depreciation matches that economic consumption to the periods benefiting from the asset.

**4. The materiality is enormous and the sensitivity confirms the economic exposure.**

The S-1 discloses that a one-year change in the average useful life of satellite assets would have an approximately **$480 million impact on operating income** for FY2025. This sensitivity demonstrates that satellite depreciation is not a minor, discretionary accounting entry — it represents a core, ongoing economic cost of operating the Connectivity business that is highly sensitive to real-world engineering and operational variables (propellant life, on-orbit performance, utilization patterns).

**5. The standard non-GAAP critique applies with unusual force here.**

Management's rationale for exclusion — that depreciation "does not directly reflect ongoing operating performance" — is the conventional justification used across industries. But for most companies, depreciating assets have multi-decade lives and the depreciation charge is a distant proxy for actual replacement spending. For Starlink, the 3-to-5-year asset life means depreciation tracks actual economic consumption far more closely. A dollar of satellite depreciation today corresponds to a dollar of replacement CapEx within a short and predictable horizon. Treating it as a non-recurring or non-operational item misrepresents the economic reality of a business that must continuously rebuild its own productive infrastructure.

### Key Takeaway

The $2,745 million EBITDA-to-operating-income gap is predominantly attributable to satellite and launch-vehicle depreciation ($2,376 million). While Adjusted EBITDA excludes depreciation as a non-cash item under standard non-GAAP convention, Starlink's 3–5 year satellite lifespans and the perpetual constellation-replenishment requirement mean that depreciation here is a close proxy for an unavoidable, economically recurring cash cost — not a distant accounting artifact. Investors relying on Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for "run-rate" earnings power should recognize that a material portion of the excluded depreciation represents near-term, non-discretionary capital replacement spending.

results.content:
## FY2025 Connectivity Segment: EBITDA-to-Operating-Income Gap

### The Numbers

From the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table in the SpaceX S-1:

| Line Item | FY2025 ($ millions) |
|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **$4,423** |
| Add: Depreciation and amortization | 2,376 |
| Add: Share-based compensation | 369 |
| Add: Restructuring charges | — |
| Add: Impairment | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

### The Implied Gap

The EBITDA-to-operating-income gap is:

**$7,168 million – $4,423 million = $2,745 million**

This $2,745 million gap is composed entirely of two adjustments:
- **Depreciation and amortization: $2,376 million** (86.6% of the gap)
- **Share-based compensation: $369 million** (13.4% of the gap)

Depreciation and amortization is by far the dominant driver of the wedge between GAAP operating income and the non-GAAP Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

---

### Why Satellite Depreciation Is Economically Recurring Even Though Excluded from Adjusted EBITDA

The S-1 defines Segment Adjusted EBITDA as segment income (loss) from operations excluding depreciation and amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairment. Depreciation is excluded under the logic that it is a non-cash expense "that management believes does not directly reflect ongoing operating performance."

However, Starlink satellite depreciation is **economically recurring** for several reasons grounded in the filing's own disclosures:

**1. Satellites have exceptionally short useful lives.**

The S-1 states that broadband satellites have a **five-year useful life** and first-generation mobile satellites have a **three-year useful life**. These are among the shortest-lived productive assets in any capital-intensive industry. The S-1 discloses that a one-year change in the average useful life of satellite assets would have an approximately **$480 million impact on operating income** for FY2025. This sensitivity demonstrates that satellite depreciation is not a minor, discretionary accounting entry — it represents a core, ongoing economic cost of operating the Connectivity business that is highly sensitive to real-world engineering and operational variables (propellant life, on-orbit performance, utilization patterns).

**5. The standard non-GAAP critique applies with unusual force here.**

Management's rationale for exclusion — that depreciation "does not directly reflect ongoing operating performance" — is the conventional justification used across industries. But for most companies, depreciating assets have multi-decade lives and the depreciation charge is a distant proxy for actual replacement spending. For Starlink, the 3-to-5-year asset life means depreciation tracks actual economic consumption far more closely. A dollar of satellite depreciation today corresponds to a dollar of replacement CapEx within a short and predictable horizon. Treating it as a non-recurring or non-operational item misrepresents the economic reality of a business that must continuously rebuild its own productive infrastructure.

### Key Takeaway

The $2,745 million EBITDA-to-operating-income gap is predominantly attributable to satellite and launch-vehicle depreciation ($2,376 million). While Adjusted EBITDA excludes depreciation as a non-cash item under standard non-GAAP convention, Starlink's 3–5 year satellite lifespans and the perpetual constellation-replenishment requirement mean that depreciation here is a close proxy for an unavoidable, economically recurring cash cost — not a distant accounting artifact. Investors relying on Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for "run-rate" earnings power should recognize that a material portion of the excluded depreciation represents near-term, non-discretionary capital replacement spending.
